ratings.rationale: The decade labels strongly anchor each set, and while a few fashion terms are specialized, most items are familiar enough to sort comfortably.
The broad medieval theme is clear, but several role words like 'cooper,' 'fletcher,' and overlapping status titles create moderate ambiguity.
Many entries are recognizable as breads, but distinguishing preparation/style groups across multiple cuisines requires more precise food knowledge.
This depends on hobby board-game literacy, and the game titles offer little help unless the player already knows the mechanics.
The revolution theme is accessible, but separating specific historical flashpoints by country demands fairly exact recall.
Most animals and collective-group cues are familiar, so the categories feel concrete and easy to infer.
Bridge terminology is technical and several items sound structurally similar, making the group boundaries easy to blur.
Many languages are familiar, but assigning them to families rather than regions requires some linguistic knowledge.
Biome categories are understandable, yet several terms are less everyday and some environments can plausibly cross categories.
The '-phobia' framing is obvious, but the Greek-derived terms require more vocabulary precision than typical easy-tier material.
Famous philosophers help, but separating them by movement and era gets tricky once the most obvious names are gone.
This relies on architecture-history knowledge, and many names are only sortable if the player already knows the styles.
Gemstones are concrete, but several stones are uncommon and players may know the item without knowing its canonical color grouping.
Everything is math-related, so the false trail density is high and the distinctions depend on formal terminology.
Olympic sports are familiar, but tying debut events to exact years adds a memory challenge beyond simple category recognition.
Heraldry vocabulary is niche and highly technical, with little everyday intuition to help separate the subtypes.
These are famous landmarks and building types that are broadly known, so most groupings are discoverable from general knowledge.
The psychology umbrella is clear, but distinguishing schools, biases, and learning models requires some textbook-style familiarity.
Basic geometry helps a lot, but 'torus' and the conic-section set make it a bit more technical than average.
The social-science terms are abstract and semantically close, so category boundaries are less obvious than the title suggests.
Nearly every item is a mental-error term, so solving depends on carefully separating adjacent academic subdomains.
Most shapes are standard school math vocabulary, with only the Platonic-solid group pushing it slightly above mid-tier ease.
Some holy books are very well known, but the less familiar secondary texts make the religious groupings noticeably harder.
Government terms are recognizable, yet some entries are broad or country-specific enough to cause moderate overlap and hesitation.
Human bone regions are concrete and taught in school, though a few smaller bone names raise the precision needed.
Kitchen-tool grouping is tangible, but several professional culinary terms are specialized and not obvious to casual players.
Internet slang is contemporary and intuitive for some players, but fast-changing usage and overlap in tone create ambiguity.
A few names are famous, but sorting psychologists by school requires real subject knowledge rather than surface recognition.
Popular science concepts are broadly familiar, and the discipline buckets are distinct enough despite a few technical terms.
Major religions are accessible, but the final two categories include terms that are broader, fuzzier, or less universally known.
This is dense formal math vocabulary with little everyday scaffolding, and several terms compete strongly within the same domain.
All entries are biological processes, so the puzzle leans on specialized terminology and fine-grained system distinctions.
Anatomy regions are fairly concrete, but the thorax and pelvis groups require more exact skeletal knowledge than basic body-part sorting.
Pasta shapes are familiar and the form-based categories are visual and intuitive, making this one of the gentler sets.
Knot names are technical and repetitive-sounding, so only players with ropework knowledge will sort them confidently.
Weather ideas are broadly recognizable, though a few less common terms like 'derecho' and 'sirocco' add some friction.
Roman gods are somewhat familiar, but the grouped domains are broad and some figures are less commonly remembered.
Forest categories are sensible, yet several subtype names are specialized and can plausibly fit more than one environmental bucket.
Film genres are everyday cultural knowledge, and most entries carry strong genre signals despite a few hybrid edge cases.
Photography terms are concrete for hobbyists, but composition, lighting, and processing vocabulary can blur for general players.
Garden plants and herbs are everyday enough that most players can sort by broad plant type without much struggle.
Election terminology is politically literate but fairly technical, with several near-neighbor systems and procedures inviting false matches.
Literary genre labels include many classroom terms that are recognizable in isolation but hard to separate precisely under pressure.
Textile patterns are visually grounded, but many names are niche or commonly confused outside fashion and design circles.
Timekeeping is a concrete theme, yet the final category mixes calendar and astronomical terminology in a less straightforward way.
Scientific laws are all formal eponymous concepts, so the puzzle is heavy on academic recall and low on intuitive sorting cues.
Tea categories are familiar and the item names often carry cultural or style signals that make grouping fairly manageable.
Many ship names are famous enough to place, and the space-shuttle group is especially easy to identify.
Musical tempo markings are specialized Italian vocabulary, and the differences between adjacent speed groups are subtle.
Beer styles are common enough for many players, though some crossover between ale, lager, and dark categories adds light confusion.
Ancient empires are standard history knowledge, and the continent-based grouping gives strong support even when one dynasty is less familiar.
Some poetry forms are famous, but several labels are literary-specialist terms that make the sets harder to distinguish cleanly.
Silent-letter patterns are taught early and the categories are direct, concrete, and low in false trails.
Sports equipment is tangible and mostly obvious from common sports knowledge, with only a few specialist accessory terms.
Oscar categories are culturally familiar, and the award-group divisions are clear even if not every exact title is top of mind.
Major rivers by continent is classic general knowledge with very strong geographic anchors and little ambiguity.
Ballet vocabulary is highly specialized and French-derived, making the move categories difficult for non-dancers to parse.
Dinosaur groups are widely recognizable to many players, and the famous examples give useful anchors for each set.
The painters and their signature works are highly famous, so the groupings are accessible from broad cultural knowledge.
Golf terms are common sports vocabulary, and the subgroups are concrete enough that even non-golfers can infer many matches.
